name: RakuAST
summary: Rakudo's RakuAST replaces the QAST-based front-end with an AST whose nodes are Raku classes. AST nodes are first-class language objects: `.AST` parses to objects, `.DEPARSE` round-trips, `IMPL-TO-QAST` lowers for execution. User macros manipulate syntax with the same tools as any Raku object hierarchy.

sections.title: Overview
sections.content:
Rakudo's RakuAST replaces the QAST-based front-end with an AST whose nodes are Raku classes under the `RakuAST::` package: `RakuAST::StatementList`, `RakuAST::Call::Name`, etc. (QAST = the older Q Abstract Syntax Tree, the runtime IR shared between NQP — "Not Quite Perl," the bootstrap subset that implements Rakudo — and Raku.)

`.AST` parses to objects, `.DEPARSE` round-trips back to source, and `IMPL-TO-QAST` lowers to QAST for execution.

sections.title: AST nodes as first-class language objects
sections.content:
The radical move: AST nodes are *first-class language objects*. User macros construct, traverse, and rewrite syntax with the same tools used to manipulate any other Raku object hierarchy. Compile-time `$?SOURCE` and `$?CHECKSUM` (SHA-1) become available for runtime debuggers and packaging.

This differs from most macro systems where the AST is a separate data type accessed through a special API. In RakuAST, the AST nodes are ordinary Raku classes; macros are Raku code operating on Raku objects. The metaprogramming surface is the same surface Raku uses for everything else.

sections.title: Trade-offs
sections.content:
The cost: every AST traversal goes through full language object dispatch, with measurable allocation pressure during compilation. RakuAST is heavier than a tagged-union AST or a flat-arena AST, both at construction time and at traversal time.

The lowering step (`IMPL-TO-QAST`) preserves the existing compiler pipeline below the AST level: RakuAST replaces the *frontend* AST while QAST remains the runtime IR. This dual structure adds a translation layer but allows incremental migration from QAST-based to RakuAST-based front-end work without invalidating the rest of the compiler.

For a language designer considering this approach: AST-as-first-class-objects is a powerful metaprogramming primitive, but only if the language design supports cheap object dispatch and the compiler can tolerate the allocation pressure.
